content:
# Acts Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 230-231 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_005_page_001.jpg, ACT_005_page_002.jpg

---

**[1]** But a man named Ananias, along with his wife Sapphira, sold a piece of property. **[2]** He kept back part of the money for himself — and his wife knew all about it — then brought the rest and laid it at the apostles' feet.

**[3]** Peter said, "Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit and keep back part of the money from the land? **[4]** Before you sold it, wasn't it yours? And after you sold it, wasn't the money at your disposal? What made you think up this scheme? You didn't lie to people — you lied to God."

**[5]** The moment Ananias heard these words, he collapsed and died. And a deep fear gripped everyone who heard about it. **[6]** The young men came forward, wrapped up his body, carried him out, and buried him.

**[7]** About three hours later, his wife came in, not knowing what had happened. **[8]** Peter confronted her: "Tell me — is this the full price you and your husband received for the land?" She said, "Yes, that was the full amount." **[9]** Peter said to her, "How could the two of you agree to test the Spirit of the Lord? Listen — the footsteps of the men who buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out too."

**[10]** Instantly she fell at his feet and died. When the young men came in, they found her dead, and they carried her out and buried her beside her husband. **[11]** A profound fear seized the entire church and everyone else who heard about these events.

**[12]** The apostles performed many signs and wonders among the people. The believers would all gather together in Solomon's Colonnade, **[13]** and although no outsider dared to associate with them, the people held them in the highest regard. **[14]** And more and more people kept being added to the Lord — crowds of both men and women — **[15]** so much so that people carried their sick out into the streets and laid them on cots and mats, hoping that when Peter walked by, at least his shadow might fall on some of them. **[16]** Crowds also poured in from the towns surrounding Jerusalem, bringing their sick and those tormented by unclean spirits, and every one of them was healed.

**[17]** Then the high priest and all his allies — the party of the Sadducees — were filled with jealousy. **[18]** They seized the apostles and threw them into the public jail. **[19]** But during the night, an angel of the Lord opened the prison doors, led them out, and said, **[20]** "Go, stand in the temple courts, and tell the people the full message of this Life."

**[21]** They obeyed, and at dawn they entered the temple and began teaching. Meanwhile, the high priest and his colleagues arrived and convened the Sanhedrin — the full council of Israel's elders — and sent orders to the prison to have the apostles brought in. **[22]** But when the officers got there, they did not find them in the jail. They returned and reported, **[23]** "We found the prison securely locked, with the guards standing at the doors — but when we opened them, there was no one inside."

**[24]** When the captain of the temple guard and the chief priests heard this report, they were baffled, wondering where this would lead. **[25]** Then someone arrived with the news: "Look — the men you put in prison are standing in the temple, teaching the people!" **[26]** So the captain went with his officers and brought them in — but without force, because they were afraid the people would stone them.

**[27]** They brought the apostles and made them stand before the Sanhedrin. The high priest interrogated them: **[28]** "We gave you strict orders not to teach in this name, and look what you've done — you've flooded Jerusalem with your teaching and you're trying to make us responsible for this man's blood!"

**[29]** Peter and the apostles answered, "We must obey God rather than human authorities. **[30]** The God of our ancestors raised Jesus — the one you had killed by hanging him on a cross. **[31]** God exalted him to his own right hand as Leader and Savior, to give Israel the chance to repent and have their sins forgiven. **[32]** We are witnesses to these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him."

**[33]** When they heard this, they were enraged and wanted to execute them. **[34]** But a Pharisee named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law who was respected by all the people, stood up in the council and ordered the men to be taken outside briefly. **[35]** Then he addressed the council: "Men of Israel, think carefully about what you are planning to do with these men. **[36]** Some time ago, Theudas appeared, claiming to be somebody important, and about four hundred men rallied behind him. He was killed, and all his followers scattered and came to nothing. **[37]** After him, Judas the Galilean appeared during the time of the census and drew people after him. He too was destroyed, and all his followers were scattered.

**[38]** "So in the present case, I tell you: stay away from these men and let them go. Because if this plan or this movement is merely human in origin, it will collapse on its own. **[39]** But if it is from God, you will never be able to stop them — and you may even find yourselves fighting against God."

They were persuaded by him. **[40]** They called the apostles back in, had them flogged, ordered them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and released them.

**[41]** The apostles left the Sanhedrin overflowing with joy — because they had been counted worthy to be dishonored for the sake of the Name. **[42]** And every day, in the temple courts and from house to house, they never stopped teaching and proclaiming the good news that the Messiah is Jesus.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 3 -- "filled your heart"**: The Greek (eplērōsen... tēn kardian) conveys Satan's total occupation of Ananias's inner will. I rendered this directly because the English idiom carries the same force -- the heart as the seat of intention, now commandeered.

2. **Verse 4 -- "What made you think up this scheme?"**: Literally "Why did you place this thing in your heart?" I shifted to natural English accusatory tone to match Peter's prosecutorial directness in the scene.

3. **Verse 11 -- "church" (ekklēsia)**: This is the first occurrence of ekklēsia in Acts. The Sinaiticus reading uses the term here to describe the believing community. I retained "church" as the dynamic equivalent for the assembled community of faith, though the word literally means "called-out assembly."

4. **Verse 20 -- "the full message of this Life"**: Greek ta rhēmata tēs zōēs tautēs -- literally "the words of this life." I rendered "full message of this Life" to capture that the angel is not asking for a partial report but the complete proclamation, and capitalizing "Life" because zōē here refers to the new, eternal life found in Christ, not merely biological existence.

5. **Verse 28 -- "you've flooded Jerusalem with your teaching"**: Literally "you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching." The verb peplērōkate (you have filled) echoes the filling language used throughout Acts (filled with the Spirit, filled with fear). "Flooded" captures the high priest's alarm at how unstoppably the message has spread.

6. **Verse 30 -- "hanging him on a cross"**: The Greek kremasantes epi xylou ("hanging on a tree/wood") deliberately echoes the curse language of Deuteronomy 21:23. I rendered "cross" rather than "tree" because the dynamic force of Peter's statement is an accusation of execution, and English readers connect "cross" immediately to crucifixion, which is the intended shock.

7. **Verse 34 -- Gamaliel**: The Sinaiticus text identifies him as a Pharisee and law-teacher honored by all the people. Historically this is Gamaliel I (Gamaliel the Elder), grandson of Hillel and Paul's own teacher (Acts 22:3). His intervention represents the moderate Pharisaic voice against Sadducean aggression.

8. **Verse 39 -- "fighting against God" (theomachoi)**: A single, powerful Greek compound word meaning "God-fighters." This is Gamaliel's climactic warning -- that opposing a divinely-originated movement makes you an enemy of God himself. The term carried tremendous rhetorical weight in the ancient world.

9. **Verse 41 -- "dishonored for the sake of the Name"**: Literally "to be dishonored on behalf of the Name." In the Sinaiticus text, "the Name" stands alone without specifying "of Jesus" -- a reverential shorthand the early church used. The apostles' joy at suffering shame is one of the most counterintuitive moments in Acts.

-- PNEUMA
